transcription_cleaned:
Doctor: Hi, how are you feeling today?
Patient: I've been feeling short of breath for a week now, and I have a low-grade fever and some chest pain on the right side.
Doctor: Okay, let me take a look at your medical history. You've been treated for bronchitis with azithromycin, but it didn't seem to help?
Patient: Yes, that's right.
Doctor: And you have a positive diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis, which you've been managing well with hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisone.
Patient: Yes, that's correct.
Doctor: And you have some other medical problems, including osteopenia, reflux disease, and hypercholesterolemia.
Patient: Yes, I take risedronate, calcium and vitamin D supplements, omeprazole, and atorvastatin for those.
Doctor: Okay, let's take a look at your examination. Your vital signs are stable, and you don't have a fever right now. Your oxygen saturation is 95% on room air.
Patient: Yes.
Doctor: You look tired, but not in any distress. When I listened to your lungs, I heard wheezes bilaterally and a few crackles at the right base.
Patient: Hmm, okay.
Doctor: Your examination is otherwise unremarkable. You don't have any synovitis in your joints, and your range of motion is normal in all joints with no muscle wasting.
Patient: That's good to hear.
Doctor: I noticed some old hypopigmented macules over your neck and face, but no skin nodules.
Patient: Yes, I've had those for a while.
Doctor: Your white count was 7.8 with neutrophils of 84%. The ESR by Westergren's method was 46 mm at the end of one hour. The comprehensive metabolic profile was normal, and the urine analysis didn't show any sediment.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: The chest radiograph showed streaky infiltrates at the right base. A high-resolution CT scan showed three lung nodules.
Patient: Oh no.
Doctor: An FDG PET scan was done, which showed avid uptake in all the nodules with an SUV value of 8.
Patient: What does that mean?
Doctor: A CT-guided lung biopsy of the right lung nodule was performed, and the lung biopsy showed fibrosis with acute and chronic inflammation and necrotizing vasculitis.
Patient: Is that serious?
Doctor: The biopsy specimen was examined for aerobic and anaerobic bacteria, acid-fast bacilli, and fungus, but it was negative on smear and culture. We also did a complete vasculitis panel, which included antinuclear antibodies, anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, and anti-SSA.
Patient: What happens next?
Doctor: We need to monitor the situation and see if there are any changes. I'll schedule follow-up appointments with you to check on your progress.
clinical_summary: A 60-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis presented with dyspnea, low-grade fever, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain, unresponsive to azithromycin. Examination revealed bilateral wheezes and right basilar crackles, with imaging showing lung nodules and PET scan indicating high uptake. Biopsy confirmed necrotizing vasculitis, and serologies supported a diagnosis of granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Treatment with methotrexate and prednisone led to symptom improvement and resolution of nodules.

differential_diagnosis.rationale: The biopsy confirmed necrotizing vasculitis and serologies supported GPA, with clinical improvement following immunosuppressive therapy.
The patient has rheumatoid arthritis, which can cause lung nodules and pleuritic pain, but the biopsy findings are more consistent with GPA.
Initial symptoms and imaging could suggest pneumonia, but lack of response to antibiotics and biopsy results make this less likely.

final_report.subjective: The patient is a 60-year-old female who presented with a one-week history of shortness of breath, low-grade fever, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. She reports that these symptoms have not improved despite a course of azithromycin, which was prescribed for presumed bronchitis. Her medical history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis, which she manages with hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisone. She also has osteopenia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypercholesterolemia, for which she takes risedronate, calcium and vitamin D supplements, omeprazole, and atorvastatin. She denies any new joint pain or swelling and has no known drug allergies.
final_report.objective: On examination, the patient appeared tired but was not in acute distress. Her vital signs were stable, with an oxygen saturation of 95% on room air. Lung auscultation revealed bilateral wheezes and crackles at the right lung base. There was no evidence of active synovitis or muscle wasting, and her joint range of motion was normal. Laboratory tests showed a white blood cell count of 7.8 with 84% neutrophils and an elevated ESR of 46 mm/h. A chest radiograph revealed streaky infiltrates at the right lung base, and a high-resolution CT scan identified three lung nodules. An FDG PET scan showed high uptake in these nodules, with an SUV of 8. A CT-guided biopsy of a right lung nodule demonstrated fibrosis, acute and chronic inflammation, and necrotizing vasculitis, with negative cultures for bacteria, mycobacteria, and fungi. Vasculitis serologies were positive for cANCA and anti-proteinase 3 antibodies.
final_report.assessment: The clinical presentation, imaging findings, and biopsy results are consistent with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), formerly known as Wegener's granulomatosis. The patient's symptoms of dyspnea, fever, and pleuritic chest pain, along with the presence of lung nodules and necrotizing vasculitis on biopsy, support this diagnosis. The positive serologies for cANCA and anti-proteinase 3 antibodies further corroborate the diagnosis of GPA. The lack of response to antibiotics and the negative cultures for infectious agents make an infectious etiology less likely. Rheumatoid arthritis-associated lung disease was considered but is less consistent with the biopsy findings.
final_report.plan: The patient has been started on methotrexate in addition to her current low-dose prednisone regimen to manage the granulomatosis with polyangiitis. She will continue her other medications for rheumatoid arthritis, osteopenia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypercholesterolemia. Follow-up appointments will be scheduled to monitor her response to treatment, with particular attention to respiratory symptoms and any potential side effects of methotrexate. Repeat imaging will be performed in three months to assess the resolution of lung nodules. The patient is advised to report any new or worsening symptoms promptly, and she will be monitored for potential complications related to immunosuppressive therapy.
